nativeRange.habitat: Outer coral reef slopes and reef-flat margins, typically over rubble and adjacent open substrate, more common on exposed outer reefs than sheltered lagoon waters
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical western and central Pacific reef habitats from Australia's Great Barrier Reef east to the Pitcairn Group, reported across roughly 3–40 m depth

diet.notes: Documented as a zooplanktivore in the wild, with small groups hovering a short distance above the reef to pick prey from the water column. In captivity, generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafood, and high-quality marine flake or pellets. Has been reported to do best with 2–4 small feedings per day, particularly for newly imported specimens; multiple sources emphasize that feeding frequency is a key driver of long-term success with this species.

avoidWith: other male fairy wrasses in the same tank

imageCandidates.notes: Debra Baker, Oqea Levu, Fiji; 2039x1711. Sharp, complete terminal-phase male showing the diagnostic blue-green head, deep red-maroon flank and greenish tail; highest resolution of the pool.

Debra Baker, Oqea Levu, Fiji; 1402x1004. Classic vivid Fiji greenback male (yellow snout, blue-green head, blue dorsal edge, red flank); slightly softer focus so kept as an alternate, not the primary.
Debra Baker, Oqea Levu, Fiji; 1066x883. Sharp female/initial-phase individual with bright orange eye-ring and faint flank lines; adds sex/phase variety to the gallery.

summary: A long-established Pacific fairy wrasse with vivid blue-green and red coloration; generally considered peaceful and reef-safe, well suited to medium-to-large mature reef aquariums with a tightly sealed lid.
careNotes: Scott's Fairy Wrasse (Cirrhilabrus scottorum) ranges across the Pacific from Australia's Great Barrier Reef east through Fiji, the Cook Islands, French Polynesia, and out to the Pitcairn Group, occupying reef-associated habitats between roughly 3 and 40 m. Three regional color forms are commonly recognized in the trade: the Fijian form (often sold as 'Greenback Fairy Wrasse') is dominated by green with a dark dorsal-fin margin; the Cook Islands form trends bluer with yellow fin accents and sharper color breaks; the Australian form carries more red into the fins with softer color transitions. The species is generally considered peaceful and reef-safe — coral and ornamental-invert nipping is not commonly reported — but individuals have been documented to be aggressive toward dottybacks, basslets, longfins, and other male fairy wrasses, so compatibility with those fish should be considered carefully when planning a stocking list. Like most members of the genus Cirrhilabrus, this species tends to be a notable jumper, and a tightly fitted lid or eurobrace is strongly advised. Best kept as a single male or as a male with one or more females added simultaneously to form a harem; introducing females after a male is established tends to provoke aggression. Individuals are protogynous (meaning they begin life as females and may later transition to male), and males display heightened nuptial coloration during courtship displays that involve swimming several meters above the substrate with fully erected fins.
breedingNotes: Captive spawning is rarely reported. In the wild the species spawns in harems with a dominant male over groups of females; replicating this social structure in a large, mature reef system may encourage nuptial display behavior but reliable rearing of fry is essentially undocumented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species page (legacy product URL — the modern /products/scotts-fairy-wrasse slug returns 404) used for color-form descriptions (Fiji greenback, Cook Islands blue, Australian red), 90 gal minimum tank, peaceful temperament, reef-safe status, and jumping warning.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed family Labridae, order Eupercaria, max length 13 cm TL, depth 3–40 m, Pacific distribution from Great Barrier Reef to Pitcairn Group, preferred temperature range 24.7–28.9°C, zooplanktivorous foraging behavior, and male courtship displays. IUCN: Least Concern.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Reports max size 5 in (~12.5 cm), minimum tank 30 gal (~120 L), temperature 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, easy-to-moderate care, protogynous hermaphroditism, sexual dichromatism (males brighter and larger), and conspecific/dottyback/basslet/longfin aggression. Significant tank-size disagreement with LiveAquaria — leaned toward the larger value per playbook rule.
